content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecimus_p113_l15"> , <gloss> expelled</gloss> (with violence); </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="emisimus_p113_l15"> , <gloss> let</gloss> [him] <gloss>go</gloss>. The words <foreign xml:lang="lat">vel</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">vel</foreign> (<gloss>or, if you like</gloss>) imply that the same act may be called by either name. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsum_p113_l15"> , <gloss>of his own accord</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="verbis_prosecuti_p113_l16"> may apply as well to kind words of dismissal as to invective. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="abiit_p113_l16">, simply, <gloss>is gone</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="excessit_p113_l16"> , <gloss>has retreated</gloss> before the storm; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="evasit_p113_l16"> , <gloss>has escaped</gloss> by stealth; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="erupit_p113_l16"> , <gloss>has broken forth</gloss> with violence, — a climax of expression, but nearly identical in sense. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="moenibus_p114_l1"> (dat. following <foreign xml:lang="lat">comparabitur</foreign>), <gloss> against</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_p114_l1"> (adding with emphasis), <gloss>and so</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hunc_quidem_p114_l1"> , <gloss>him at any rate</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sine_controversia_p114_l2"> , <gloss>without dispute unquestionably</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="versabitur_p114_l3"> , <gloss>will be busy</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="campo_p114_l4"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="foro_p114_l4"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="curia_p114_l4"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="parietes_p114_l4">: observe the narrowing climax. <pb n="380"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="loco_est_p114_l5"> , <gloss>he lost his vantage-ground</gloss>: a military expression, hence the simple abl.; § <ref n="AG 428">428</ref>, f (258, a, N.2); cf. B. 229, I; G. 390, 2, N.2; H. 463 (414, ii); H-B. 408, 2, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nullo_impediente_p114_l6">, i.e. his defenders till now could screen him by forms of law. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="justum_p114_l7"> (if retained in the text), <gloss>regular, in due form</gloss>; cf. note on latrocinio, p. 109,1. I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_etc_p114_l9">: § <ref n="AG 572">572</ref>, b (333, b); B. 331, V, a; G. 542; H. 588, i (540, iv, N.); H.-B. 594, c. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cruentum_p114_l10"> (pred.), <gloss>reeking with blood</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivis_nobis_p114_l10"> (abl. abs.), <gloss>leaving us alive</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civis_p114_l12">: acc. plur. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jacet_p114_l13">, etc., <gloss>lies prostrate</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="retorquet_oculos_p114_l15"> begins the figure of a wild beast, which is continued in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="faucibus_p114_l15"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="profecto_p114_l15"> , <gloss>no doubt</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quae_quidem_p114_l16"> , <gloss>which really</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_projecerit_p114_l17">: see note on </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_extulit_p114_l17">, 1.9, above; for mood, see § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>,3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 541; H. 588, ii (516, ii); H.-B. 535, 2, a. <milestone n="2" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="For_followingsection_p114_l19">, cf. Cat. 1, sects. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.27">27</ref>, <ref n="Cic. Catil. 2.28">28</ref> where the supposed complaint against Cicero for not having put Catiline to death and his reply to it are given at greater length. </gloss>
lemma:
For the contents of this and the following
								section

content: <gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qualis_omnis_p114_l19">: acc. plur. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p114_l19">: § <ref n="AG 522">522</ref>, a (3", c); B. 304, 3, a; G. 254, R.2; H. 583 (511, I, N.1); H.-B. 582, 3, a. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="qui_accuset_p114_l20"> , <gloss>as to accuse</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 535">535</ref> (320); B. 283, I ; G. 631,2; H. 591, I (503, i); H-B. 521, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ista_p114_l22">: for gender, see § <ref n="AG 296">296</ref>, a (195, d); cf. B. 250,3; G. 211, R.5; H. 396, 2 (445, 4); H.-B. 326, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="interfectum_esse_p114_l23">: § <ref n="AG 486">486</ref>, b, N. (288, d); B. 270, 2, a; G. 280, R.2; H.-B. 582, 3, a, footnote 2; observe the emphatic position. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="oportebat_p114_l24">: for tense, see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl100.13">p. 100, l. 13</ref> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hujus_imperi_p114_l25">: see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl104.16">p. 104, l. 16</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_publica_p114_l25"> , <gloss>the public interest</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_multos_p114_l27">, etc.: the passages in brackets are probably spurious; it will be observed that they merely repeat —the preceding statement in each case. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_p115_l1"> (causal) <foreign xml:lang="lat">viderem</foreign>, <gloss>seeing</gloss>: its obj. is </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_possem_p115_l1"> (which is the apod. of </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_multassem_p115_l1">) ; § <ref n="AG 569">569</ref>, a (288, f); B. 270,3; G. 248; H. 619, 2 (537,3); H-B. 472, c </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_probata_p115_l1">: nearly equivalent to <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum ne vos quidem</foreign>. . . <foreign xml:lang="lat">probaretis</foreign>: implying that if <emph> they</emph> do not sustain the act, much less will the people at large. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="multassem_p115_l2">: for fut. perf. of direct; § <ref n="AG 589">589</ref>, 3 (337, 3); B. 319, B; G. 657, 5; H. 646 (527, i). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fore_ut_p115_l2"> , <gloss>the result would be that</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="at_possetis_p115_l4">, result clause explaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="huc_p115_l4"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="videretis_p115_l5">: § <ref n="AG 593">593</ref> (342); B. 324, I G. 663, I; H. 652 (529, ii); H.-B. 539; if not dependent on <foreign xml:lang="lat"> possetis</foreign>, it would be videbetis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quem_quidem_p115_l5"> , <gloss>whom, by the way</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="intellegatis_p115_l6">: § <ref n="AG 565">565</ref> (331, i); B. 295, 6; G. 553, 2; H. 564, ii, I (499, 3); H-B. 531, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_exierit_p115_l7">: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3(341, d); B. 323; G. 539; H. 588, ii (5I6, ii); H-B. 535, 2, N.2. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_p115_l9">: eth. dat.; as if, <gloss>I notice</gloss>; § <ref n="AG 380">380</ref> (236); B. 188, 2, b; G. 351; 11.432 (389); H.-B. 372. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aes_alienum_p115_l10">, etc., i.e. petty debts run up in cook-shops and the like; not like the heavy mortgages spoken of afterwards. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="reliquit_p115_l11">: notice the emphatic position. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quos_viros_p115_l12">: for a characterization of Catiline's partisans, see sects. 18-23. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="prae_p115_l14"> , <gloss>in comparison with</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Gallicanis_p115_l14">, i.e. those permanently stationed in Cisalpine Gaul. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">ager Gallicus</foreign> below was that strip of sea-coast north of Picenum formerly occupied by the Senones, but at this time reckoned a part of Umbria. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hoc_dilecta_p115_l15"> refers to a levy recently raised. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Q_Metellus_p115_l15"> (Celer): see note on Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.19">19</ref> , <ref n="pl107.13">p. 107, l. 13</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="senibus_p115_l17">, etc., i.e. those classes who naturally look forward to a revolution to mend their fortunes. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="luxuria_p115_l18"> = <gloss> high-livers</gloss>: abstract for concrete, as common in Latin and older English; Cf. Shakespeare, <title>All's Well</title> ii. I, 91: "Bring in the admiration"(i.e. this wonderful person). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vadimonia_deserere_p115_l19"> , <gloss>desert</gloss> their bondsmen, i.e. leave them in the lurch in their creditors' suits. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="edictum_praetoris_p115_l21">, in effect like a <gloss>sheriff's writ</gloss>. Any official order of a magistrate was an <foreign xml:lang="lat">edictum</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hos_p115_l22">, as opposed to those he did take out. <pb n="382"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fulgent_purpura_p115_l24">, i.e displaying their rank as Senators, who alone had the right to wear the broad purple stripe (<foreign xml:lang="lat">latus clavus</foreign>) on the tunic. The reference, therefore, is to foppish young nobles. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mallem_p115_l24">: § <ref n="AG 447">447</ref>, I (311, b); B. 280, 4; G. 258, and N.1; H. 556 (486, i); H.-B. 519, I, b. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eduxisset_p115_l24">: § <ref n="AG 565">565</ref>(331, f a.); B. 295, 8; G. 546, R.2; H. 565, 2 (499, 2); H.-B. 5;9, I, c. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_permanent_p115_l24">: a future condition; § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, a, N. (307, a, N.); G. 228; H. 533, 2 (467, 5); H.-B. 571. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mementote_p115_l25">, i.e. let them remember that they are objects of suspicion and shall be watched accordingly. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="atque_hoc_p115_l27">, etc., i.e. their effrontery makes them still more a cause for alarm. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="video_p115_l28">, i.e. I know perfectly well. - </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cui_sit_p115_l29">, etc.: cf. Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.9">9</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="superioris_noctis_p115_l31">, i.e. three nights ago. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ne_p116_l3"> , <gloss> surely</gloss>: an affirmative particle sometimes wrongly spelled <foreign xml:lang="lat">nae</foreign>. <milestone n="116.5" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_videretis_p116_l3">: clause of result explaining </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p116_l3"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nisi_vero_p116_l7">: ironical (as usual), introducing a <foreign xml:lang="lat">reductio ad absurdunt</foreign>. (The </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_p116_l7"> only doubles that in <foreign xml:lang="lat">nisi</foreign>.) </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_jam_p116_l8"> , <gloss>no longer</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Aurelia__via__p116_l11">: see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.24">24</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="rem_publicam_p116_l13">: § <ref n="AG 397">397</ref>, d (240, d); B. 183; G. 343, 1 H. 421 (381); H.-B. 399 </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sentinam_p116_l13"> , <gloss> refuse</gloss> (see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl104.22">p. 104, l. 22</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ejecerit_p116_l14">: the conclusion is implied in </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="O_fortunatam_p116_l14"> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exhausto_p116_l14"> , <gloss>drained off</gloss> (cf. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sentina_p116_l14"> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="recreata_p116_l15"> , <gloss> invigorated</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="toti_Itali_p116_l17">: § <ref n="AG 429">429</ref>, 2 (258,1 2); B. 228, I, b; G. 388; H. 485, I (425, 2); H.-B. 436, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subjector_p116_l18"> , <gloss> forger</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="circumscriptor_p116_l18"> , <gloss> swindler</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perditus_p116_l21"> , <gloss>abandoned wretch</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hosce_p116_l22">: § <ref n="AG 146">146</ref>, a, N.1 (101, footnote); B. 87, footnote 2; G. 104, i, N 1; H. 178, 2 (186, I); H.-B. 138, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="asciverit_p117_l3">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 485">485</ref>, c (287, c); B. 268, 6; G. 513; H. 550 (495, vi); H.-B. 481. <milestone n="5" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possitis_p117_l4">: § <ref n="AG 532">532</ref> (317, c); B. 282, 4; G. 545, R.3; Cf. H. 568, 4 (499, 2, N.) ; H.-B. 502, 2, c. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="diversa_studia_p117_l4">. In another passage (<ref n="Cic. Cael. 13">Cael. 13</ref>) Cicero ascribes to Catiline: <foreign xml:lang="lat">Cum tristibus severe, cum remissis jucunde, cum senibus graviter, cum juventute comiter, cum facinorosis audaciter, cum libidinosis luxuriose vivere</foreign> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="in_ratione_p117_l4"> , <gloss>in different directions</gloss>. <pb n="383"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ludo_p117_l5">, the regular <gloss>training-school</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatorio_p117_l5">: see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl110.31">p. 110, l. 31</ref>, and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="levior_p117_l7">, etc.: the Roman actors, though some of them achieved distinction, were generally regarded as a low class of men. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p117_l8">, i.e. though a companion of such dissolute persons, yet he possessed the qualities of fortitude and endurance so much admired by the Romans. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exercitatione_p117_l9"> (abl. of means), etc., <gloss>trained by the practice of debaucheries and crimes to endure</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="frigore_perferendis_p117_l9">: abl. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adsuefactus_p117_l9">; § <ref n="AG 507">507</ref>, N.1 (301, N.); G. 431; cf. H-B. 612, iv, 431. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fortis_p117_l10"> , <gloss>a strong and able fellow</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="istis_p117_l10"> , <gloss>those creatures:</gloss> § <ref n="AG 297">297</ref>, c (102, c); B. 246, 4; G. 306, N.; H. 507, 3 (450, i, N.); H.-B. 274, 4. 117 11 <foreign xml:lang="lat">cum</foreign> . . . <foreign xml:lang="lat"> consumeret</foreign> (not concessive), <gloss>while consuming</gloss> . </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="subsidia_p117_l10">, etc., i.e. means (his uncommon powers of body and mind) which might have been used, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="audaciae_p117_l17"> , <gloss>acts of audacity</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obligaverunt_p117_l19"> , <gloss> encumbered</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="res_p117_l19"> , <gloss> property</gloss>; fides, <gloss>credit</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="libido_p117_l21">, i.e. luxurious habits and tastes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quidem_p117_l23"> (concessive), <gloss>no doubt</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="homines_p117_l24"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="viris_p117_l24">: observe the difference in sense. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="mihi_p117_l26">: the ethical dat. gives the phrase a familiar and contemptuous turn which may be reproduced in English by <gloss>forsooth</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="obliti_p117_l28">: observe the quantity. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="caedem_p117_l29">, etc.: notice the strong contrast between the character of these worn-out debauchees and the sanguinary nature of their threats. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="instare_p117_l31"> , <gloss>is close at hand</gloss>; plane merely emphasizes the idea of the verb. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="propagarit_p118_l2">: for tense, see § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, c, N. (307, c, a.) ; G. 595, N.2; H. 540(473). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pertimescamus_p118_l3"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possit_p118_l3">: subj. of characteristic. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="unius_p118_l5">: Pompey, just returning from his triumphs in the East. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quacumque_ratione_p118_l10">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">fieri potest</foreign> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="resecanda_erunt_p118_l11"> , <gloss>shall need the knipe</gloss> (lit. <gloss>must be cut away</gloss>): the figure is derived from surgery. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_permanent_p118_l12">: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, a, N. (307, a, N.); G. 228; H. 533, 2 (467,5); H.-B. 572. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="exspectent_p118_l13">: hort. subj. in apod.; § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d (307, d); B. 305, 2 G. 595; H. 580 (508, 4); H.-B. 532,1. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="etiam_p118_l14"> , <gloss> still</gloss> (after all that has been done). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p118_l15">, obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">adsequi</foreign>, <gloss>if I could effect it</gloss> (referring to </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ipsos_p118_l15">, etc.), i.e. their expulsion. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="enim_p118_l17">, i.e. the idea is absurd, as is implied in the irony following. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quid_p118_l19"> , <gloss>tell me</gloss>: i.e. "is that possible ?" in view of the circumstances, which he proceeds to narrate. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hesterno_die_p118_l19"> qualifies </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="convocavi_p118_l19"> . </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="detuli_p118_l21">: technical term for laying a matter before the Senate; cf. <foreign xml:lang="lat">referre</foreign> (ad <foreign xml:lang="lat"> senatum</foreign>) in the Vocabulary. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quaesivi_p118_l28">, etc.: see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.9">9</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="necne_p118_l29">: § <ref n="AG 335">335</ref>,d(211,d); B. 162,4; G.459; H.380, 1 (353, N.1); H.-B. 234, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_p118_l32">: dat. of agent; § <ref n="AG 375">375</ref> (232, a); B. 189, 2; G. 354; H. 431, 2 (388, I); H-B. 373, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="teneretur_p119_l1"> , <gloss>was caught</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pararet_p119_l2">: for pluperf. (see note on Cat. 1, <ref n="pl100.13">p. 100, l. 13</ref> ). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="securis_p119_l2"> , </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fascis_p119_l2">: the use of these signified that Catiline intended to assume the authority and <foreign xml:lang="lat">imperium</foreign> of consul (see Fig. 25, p.290). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aquilam_p119_l3">: see Cat. 1, <ref n="pl109.6">p. 109, l. 6</ref>, and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eiciebam_p119_l5">: conative imperf.; § <ref n="AG 471">471</ref>, c (277, c); B. 260, 3; G. 233; H. 534, 2 (469, I); H.-B. 484. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="credo_p119_l6">: ironical, as very often in this parenthetical use. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="suo_nomine_p119_l8">, i.e. not by Catiline's order; the whole is, of course, ironical, as is already indicated by credo. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Massiliam_p119_l10"> : <gloss> Marseilles</gloss>, an ancient Greek city of Gaul, always faithful and friendly to Rome. It was a favorite place of sojourn for Romans who went into voluntary exile. <milestone n="119.11" unit="pg_l"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="condicionem_p119_l10"> , <gloss> terms</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nunc_p119_l12"> , <gloss>even now</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="pertimuerit_p119_l14"> , <gloss>take alarm</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spe_conatuque_p119_l18">: referring of course to his treasonable hopes and designs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_tanti_p119_l22"> , <gloss>it is worth my while</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 417">417</ref> (252, a); cf. B. 203, 3; G. 380, I, R.; H. 448 (404); H.-B. 356, I. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="depellatur_p119_l25">: § <ref n="AG 528">528</ref>(314); B. 310,ii; G.573; H. 587(313,i); H.-B. 529. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sane_p119_l25"> (concessive), <gloss>f you like</gloss> (see Vocab.). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="invidiae_p119_l28">, etc.: rather than have his predictions verified in this way, Cicero prefers the unjust odium of having arbitrarily driven Catiline to exile. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="aliquando_p119_l31"> , <gloss> some</gloss> day. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_emiserim_p119_l31">: § <ref n="AG 592">592</ref>, 3 (341, d); B. 323; G. 541; H. 588, ii (516, ii); H-B. 535, 2, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="emiserim_p119_l31"> , </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="eiecerim_p119_l31"> , <gloss>let him go... drove him out</gloss> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_interfectus_p120_l2">, etc.: he thus adroitly excuses himself to those who would have preferred harsher measures. Notice the identity in sound in <foreign xml:lang="lat">profectus</foreign>, <foreign xml:lang="lat"> interfectus</foreign>, and observe how the argument <foreign xml:lang="lat">a fortiori</foreign> is brought out by the exact antithesis. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_p120_l3"> (corrective), <gloss>and yet</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dictitant_p120_l4">: notice the <foreign xml:lang="lat">frequentative</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="nemo_p120_l5"> , <gloss>not a man</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="misericors_p120_l5">: his going to Manlius was his inevitable ruin, and yet, for all their pity, they still wished him to go. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="latrocinantem_p120_l8"> , <gloss>in partisan warfare</gloss> (see note on p.109, L I). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivere_p120_l8">: § <ref n="AG 583">583</ref>, c (336, c, N.2) ; G. 644, R3, b; cf. H. 613, 7 (535, i, 6); H.-B. 535, I, c. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vivis_nobis_p120_l10">, i.e. without assassinating me. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sanare_p120_l17">: cf. note on <foreign xml:lang="lat">vivere</foreign>, 1.8, above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sibi_p120_l17"> , <gloss>for their own good</gloss>: for reflexive, see § <ref n="AG 301"> 301</ref>, b, N. (I 96, c, N.); G. 520. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="placare_p120_l17"> , <gloss>gain aver</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="comparentur_p120_l20"> , <gloss>are made up</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="singulis_p120_l20"> , <gloss>to them one by one</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="si_quam_p120_l21">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">adferre</foreign>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="est_eorum_p120_l22"> , <gloss>consists of those</gloss> (pred. gen.). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="possessiones_p120_l23"> , <gloss>landed property</gloss>. <pb n="386"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="dissolvi_p120_l24">, sc. <foreign xml:lang="lat">a possessionibus</foreign>: i.e. although they might pay their debts by the sale of their estates, they cannot make up their minds to do so. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="voluntas_causa_p120_l25"> , <gloss>their purposes and claims</gloss>, i.e. their position before the world. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tu_p120_l26">: the use of the singular, as if he were addressing one of these men directly, gives point to his reproach of the whole class. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sis_p120_l27">: § <ref n="AG 444">444</ref> (268); B. 277; G. 466; H. 5591 4 (484, v); H.-B. 503. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tuas_p120_l30">: emphatic. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tabulas_novas_p120_l30"> , <gloss>new accounts</gloss>, i.e. a general scaling down of debts by legislative enactment, such as that, <date when="-0086">B.C. 86</date>, "which reduced every private claim to the fourth part of its nominal amount, and cancelled three-fourths in favor of the debtors." </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctionariae_p121_l2">: a forced sale of their estates would give them "new accounts" (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tabulae</foreign>) by reducing their debts; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="auctionarinae_p121_l2"> [<foreign xml:lang="lat">tabulae</foreign>] would be the <gloss> placards</gloss> advertising the sale in question. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p121_l4">, obj. of <foreign xml:lang="lat">facere</foreign>, relating to the forced sale. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_p121_l4"> , <gloss>and not</gloss>, connects <foreign xml:lang="lat">facere</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">certare</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="certare_usuris_p121_l5"> , <gloss>struggle to meet the interest</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 413"> 413</ref>, b (248, b); H. (419, II); H.-B. 419, 4. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fructibus_p121_l5">: abl. of means. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="uteremur_p121_l6"> , <gloss>we should find</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hosce_p121_l7">: more emphatic than <foreign xml:lang="lat">hos</foreign>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="o_facturi_121"> , <gloss>likely to offer prayers</gloss>, i.e. they will confine themselves to sympathizing with Catiline's revolt; no active cooperation with him need be feared from them. <milestone n="9" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="o_facturi_121"> , <gloss>likely to offer prayers</gloss>, i.e. they will confine themselves to sympathizing with Catiline's revolt; no active cooperation with him need be feared from them. <milestone n="9" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="premuntur_p121_l11">: notice the emphasis,—this class is insolvent; the former class is heavily in debt, but has resources. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quieta_publica_p121_l13">: no poor man could hope to gain political prominence at Rome in ordinary times; these men therefore look to anarchy to achieve their political ends. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scilicet_p121_l15"> , <gloss>in fact</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="desperent_p121_l16"> , <gloss>have no hope</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_vigilare_p121_l17">, etc., indir. disc. dependent on the idea of saying implied in <foreign xml:lang="lat">praecipiendum</foreign>: § <ref n="AG 580">580</ref>, a (336, N.2); G. 652, R.2; H. 642, I (523, i, N.); H.-B. 589, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="magnos_animos_p121_l18">: see Vocab. under animus. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="praesentis_p121_l22"> agrees with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="deos_p121_l22"> : <gloss>will be at hand, and</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_si_p121_l23"> , <gloss>now if</gloss> (as often). The </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p121_l23"> is merely adverbial acc.: § <ref n="AG 397">397</ref>, a (240, b); B. 185, 2; G. 610, R.2; H. 416, 2 (378, 2); cf. H.-B. 388, a, N.; not like </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p121_l23_2"> in 1.4, above. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="jam_p121_l23"> , <gloss>at once</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sint_adepti_p121_l23">: fut. cond. less vivid. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cum_furore_p121_l23">: § <ref n="AG 412">412</ref>, a (248, N.); G. 399; 14.473, 31 N. (419, iii, N.1); H.-B. 445, 3. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_vident_p121_l26"> , <gloss>don't they see?</gloss> § <ref n="AG 332">332</ref>, a (210, b); B. 162, 2, d; G. 453; H. 378, I (351, 3); H.-B. 231, I, a. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="adepti_sint_p121_l27">, for the fut. perf. indic. of the direct disc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="fugitivo_p121_l27">, i.e. one of their own slaves; for, when law is overthrown, brute force will control all. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="sit_necesse_p121_l28">: § <ref n="AG 516">516</ref>, d (307, d); G. 595; H. 580 (508, 4); cf. H.-B. 582, I. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_coloniis_p121_l31">: Sulla rewarded his veterans (120,000 in number) by liberal grants of land, partly in <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipia</foreign> already existing, partly in new colonies which he founded for them. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="universas_p122_l1"> , <gloss>as a whole</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="civium_esse_p122_l1"> , <gloss>consist of</gloss> etc. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ei_coloni_p122_l2"> , <gloss>these are colonists of this sort</gloss> (as opposed to the general character of the colonies, which Cicero does not wish to impugn). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="beati_p122_l5"> , <gloss>men of wealth</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="Sulla_p122_l7">, etc., <gloss>Sulla will have to be raised from the dead</gloss>, for they can have no such hope in Catiline. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="agrestis_p122_l8"> , <gloss> farmers</gloss>, not Sulla's colonists. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="veterum_p122_l9">: alluding to the plunder of the disorderly times following Sulla's victory over the Marian party. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illorum_temporum_p122_l12">, i.e. the times of proscription. <milestone n="10" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vacillant_p122_l18"> , <gloss>stagger under</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vadimoniis_p122_l18">, etc.: the three steps in bankruptcy,—<gloss>bail, judgment, and sale of property</gloss>; <foreign xml:lang="lat">proscriptio</foreign> is strictly the public notice that property is for sale. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="infitiatores_lentos_p122_l21"> , <gloss>dilatory debtors</gloss> (lit. <gloss>deniers</gloss>, i.e. persons who avoid payment of their debts by every possible subterfuge). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="stare_p122_l22"> , <gloss>keep their feet</gloss> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ita_p122_l23"> , <gloss>in such a way</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_modo_p122_l23">, etc.: § <ref n="AG 217">217</ref>, e (149, c); B. 343, 2, a; G. 482,5, R.1; H. 656, 3 (552, 2); H.-B. 299. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="non_revoco_p122_l29">: § <ref n="AG 467">467</ref> (276, b); B. 259, 2; G. 233; H. 530 (467, 6); H.-B. 484. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="carcer_p123_l1">: this is the <foreign xml:lang="lat">Tullianum</foreign>, a dungeon near the Forum, still existing. It was properly a jail for temporary detention, as imprisonment was not recognized in Rome as a form of punishment. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="numero_p123_l2"> , <gloss>in order</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="genere_p123_l2"> , <gloss>in rank</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="imberbis_p123_l5">: a mark of effeminacy; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bene_barbatos_p123_l5"> , <gloss> full-bearded</gloss>, doubtless a military affectation, as, until lately, the wearing of a mustache. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="velis_p123_l6"> , <gloss> veils</gloss>, rather than the substantial <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign>, which was of unbleached wool. The whole description suggests foppishness and effeminacy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="saltare_cantare_p123_l11">, these accomplishments were hardly regarded as respectable by the better classes. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="spargere_p123_l11">, i.e. in food or drink: poisoning has in all ages been carried to a high art in Italy. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="scitote_p123_l13">: notice the second (fut.) imperat. (regularly used in this word). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_noctibus_p123_l16">: although this was spoken Nov. 9, yet the Roman year was at this time in such a state of confusion that the true date was probably some time in December, just when the winter was setting in.</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbes_coloniarum_p123_l27">, etc.: the colonies and free communities (</gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="municipia_p123_l27">) included the walled cities (</gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="urbes_p123_l27">) in their territory. These well-manned walls would be more than a match for Catiline's rude works (<foreign xml:lang="lat">tumulis</foreign> ). </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="causas_p124_l1">, i.e. the cause of the conspirators and that of the state in their moral aspect (cf. in <foreign xml:lang="lat">eius modi</foreign>, etc., 1.12, below). </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ex_ipso_p124_l2"> , <gloss>from the very comparison</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="bona_ratio_p124_l10"> , <gloss>good counsel</gloss>; </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="perdita_p124_l10"> , <gloss> desperate</gloss>. <milestone n="12" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="custodiis_vigilisque_p124_l17">: see Cat. 1, sect. <ref n="Cic. Catil. 1.8">8</ref> and note. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="consultum_p124_l19">, etc., <gloss> provident</gloss> measures have been taken. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="coloni_municipesque_p124_l19">: a colony differed from a <foreign xml:lang="lat">municipium</foreign> in being founded by Roman (or Latin) citizens, who retained from the first their citizenship, either in whole or in part. By Cicero's time there was no longer any real difference between the two classes of towns; but the colonies always retained a certain precedence in rank. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="hac_excursione_p124_l20">: see Introd., p. 113 of text. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="gladiatores_p124_l22">: see <ref n="pl117.5">p. 117, l. 5</ref>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quamquam_p124_l23"> (corrective), referring to <foreign xml:lang="lat">manum certissimam</foreign> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="tamen_p124_l24">: pointing the contrast between the suppression of this body and Catiline's expectations from them. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="vocari_videtis_p124_l29">: the members of the Senate had their gathering place (<foreign xml:lang="lat">senaculum</foreign>) adjoining the <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign>, and were summoned by heralds (<foreign xml:lang="lat">praecones</foreign>) from this into the building. If any were absent, the heralds were sent to their houses. The <foreign xml:lang="lat">curia</foreign> and <foreign xml:lang="lat">senaculum</foreign> were visible from the place of assembly in the Forum, and the heralds could no doubt be seen going their rounds. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="monitos_volo_p125_l2">: 497, c, N. (292, d, N.); G. 537; H.-B. 605, 3. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="solutior_p125_l3">: for compar., see § <ref n="AG 291">291</ref>, a (93, a); B. 240, I G. 297; H. 498 (444, I); H.-B. 241, 2. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quod_p125_l4">, etc., <gloss>as for the rest</gloss>, i.e. what remains to be done. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="horum_p125_l5"> and </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="his_p125_l5"> relate to the citizens by whom he is surrounded, and imply a gesture. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="cujus_p125_l8">: referring, like <foreign xml:lang="lat">qui</foreign>, to the subject of <foreign xml:lang="lat">sentiet</foreign>. <milestone n="13" unit="chapter"/> </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="me_p125_l17">, etc., abl. abs. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="togato_p125_l17"> , <gloss>in perfect peace</gloss>, i.e. without any military demonstration: the <foreign xml:lang="lat">toga</foreign> was the regular dress of the Roman in time of peace. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="illud_p125_l22">: in appos. with </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="ut_possitis_p125_l22">: I <gloss>will secure that</gloss>, etc. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="neque_que_p125_l24"> , <gloss>not... and</gloss>. </gloss>
<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quibus_ducibus_p125_l28"> , <gloss>under whose guidance</gloss>. </gloss>

<gloss xmlns="http://www.tei-c.org/ns/1.0" xml:id="quam_hanc_p126_l3"> , <gloss>this city which</gloss>: § <ref n="AG 307">307</ref>, b, N. (200, b, N.); G. 616; H. 399, 5(445, 9); or (repeating the noun) <gloss>the city which</gloss>, etc.,— <gloss> <emph>that</emph> city</gloss>. </gloss>
